skill.name: when-bridging-web-cli-use-web-cli-teleport
skill.description: Guide for building a web-to-CLI bridge that lets browser interfaces trigger CLI workflows and display command results.
skill.summary: Connect browser interfaces to CLI workflows with REST and WebSocket bridge patterns.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many execution, network, and filesystem indicators. Review confirms the highest-risk issue: the skill teaches an unauthenticated web endpoint that builds a shell command from request data and passes it to child_process.exec. Several weak-crypto and path-traversal alerts are false positives from documentation text and relative paths, and no prompt-injection attempt was found.

high_findings.title: Unauthenticated Web-to-Shell Command Execution
high_findings.description: The bridge server accepts command and args from the request body, concatenates them into a shell string, and executes the result with child_process.exec. This is a direct remote command execution pattern if the generated server is exposed or reachable by a browser client.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is explicit: request-controlled command data is transformed into a shell command and passed to exec. The surrounding files document the same web-to-exec design rather than an isolated example.

high_findings.title: Permissive Network Bridge Lacks Authentication Controls
high_findings.description: The sample server enables permissive CORS and WebSocket communication while exposing command execution and UI rendering endpoints. The text later recommends authentication, but the provided implementation does not enforce it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The server code shows open CORS and unauthenticated endpoints in the same component that runs CLI commands. The mitigation is only mentioned as advice, not implemented in the runnable sample.

medium_findings.title: Shell and Request Body Interpolation in CLI Helper
medium_findings.description: The helper script interpolates component and data values into a curl request body and runs analysis through command substitution. This can corrupt requests or create injection risk if untrusted values reach those variables.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly interpolates shell variables into a request body and captures command output with command substitution. The example uses local values, so exploitability depends on how users adapt it.

medium_findings.title: Deployment Instructions Can Expose the Unsafe Bridge
medium_findings.description: The workflow starts the bridge server in the background and provides Docker deployment steps that publish port 3001. Following these steps can make the unsafe command execution API reachable beyond a local test.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deployment commands are explicit and publish the bridge port. The risk is tied to the earlier unauthenticated exec endpoint, so this is a confirmed amplification path.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings point to markdown headings, labels, and ordinary words, not MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other cryptographic algorithm usage. No evidence of cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive text or workflow labels. They do not contain crypto APIs or algorithm names used for security decisions.

low_findings.title: Static Path-Traversal Alerts Are Mostly Benign Relative Paths
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are relative path examples used to create a sibling directory or import a local server in tests. They are not shown processing user-controlled paths, although they still reflect filesystem interaction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows fixed relative paths. There is no evidence that external input is joined into these paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.exec With Request-Controlled Shell String
dangerous_patterns.description: The implementation constructs fullCommand from request fields and executes it through child_process.exec.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path from request body to exec is direct and visible in the sample implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard CORS on Command Bridge
dangerous_patterns.description: The bridge initializes Socket.IO with wildcard CORS and also enables Express CORS before defining command execution routes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard origin is explicit. Its impact is high because it appears in the same server that exposes command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Helper Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script captures analyzer output using shell command substitution and forwards it to the web bridge.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present, but the sample value is a local path. Risk increases when users adapt the helper for untrusted inputs.

content.user_title: Bridge Web Apps to CLI Workflows
content.value_statement: Teams often need browser controls for command-line automation, but direct web-to-shell bridges are difficult to design safely. This skill outlines REST, WebSocket, and deployment patterns for connecting web interfaces to CLI workflows.
actual_capabilities: Defines a phased workflow for designing a web and CLI bridge architecture.
Shows REST endpoints for command execution and web UI rendering.
Includes React, Express, Socket.IO, Docker, and test examples.
Provides quick start and full process documentation for the bridge workflow.
Documents success criteria, performance targets, and common operational issues.
limitations: The sample command execution endpoint is unsafe without major security changes.
Authentication, authorization, command allowlisting, and input validation are described but not implemented.
The workflow depends on Node.js, npm, Docker, and claude-flow tooling.
It is a guidance skill, not a maintained production bridge package.

use_cases.title: Prototype a CLI Operations Dashboard
use_cases.description: Create a local web interface that starts approved CLI workflows and displays their output during development.

use_cases.title: Plan a Web-to-Tool Architecture
use_cases.description: Map REST and WebSocket flows before building a controlled bridge between a browser app and command-line tools.

use_cases.title: Review Bridge Security Requirements
use_cases.description: Identify controls needed before exposing command execution through a browser or API surface.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Bridge Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain the main components of a web-to-CLI bridge and list the security controls I must add before testing it.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Local Prototype Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a local-only prototype for a web interface that runs one allowlisted CLI command and streams output to the browser.

prompt_templates.title: Harden the Sample Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to rewrite the bridge design with authentication, command allowlisting, argument validation, timeouts, and audit logging.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review a proposed web-to-CLI bridge architecture and produce a production readiness checklist with blocking risks.

output_examples.input: Plan a local prototype for a browser button that runs a safe status command.
output_examples.output: A staged implementation plan that limits the bridge to localhost, uses one allowlisted command, streams output, and lists required validation checks.

output_examples.input: Review the sample bridge for security risks.
output: Remote command execution risk from request-controlled shell commands.
Missing authentication and authorization on bridge endpoints.
Recommended changes: command allowlist, structured arguments, timeouts, logging, and network restrictions.

output_examples.input: Create deployment criteria for this bridge.
output_examples.output: A release checklist covering network exposure, identity controls, command policy, monitoring, rate limits, and rollback requirements.

best_practices: Keep the bridge local until authentication, authorization, and command allowlisting are implemented.
Use structured command arguments and avoid shell string construction for command execution.
Add timeouts, rate limits, audit logs, and monitoring before any shared deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not expose a browser-accessible endpoint that passes request text directly to a shell.
Do not rely on wildcard CORS or unauthenticated WebSocket access for command workflows.
Do not deploy the sample server as production code without a security redesign.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps plan and prototype a bridge between browser interfaces and command-line workflows using REST and WebSocket patterns.

faq.question: Is the provided sample safe for production?
faq.answer: No. The sample needs authentication, authorization, command allowlisting, validation, and safer process execution before production use.

faq.question: Which tools does the workflow reference?
faq.answer: It references React, Express, Socket.IO, Docker, npm, curl, and claude-flow commands.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is request-controlled command execution through a web API.

faq.question: How should teams use this skill safely?
faq.answer: Use it as architecture guidance, then replace unsafe command execution with allowlisted actions and strict access controls.
